skill.name: webapp-testing
skill.description: Test local web applications with Python Playwright scripts, screenshots, console logs, DOM inspection, and optional server lifecycle management.
skill.summary: Guides Playwright-based testing and debugging for local web applications.
skill.author: ZhanlinCui

security_audit.summary: Static findings for weak cryptography are false positives; the cited lines contain description text and argparse setup, not cryptographic algorithms. The skill still has confirmed high-risk behavior because it encourages black-box script execution and includes a helper that runs user-supplied server commands through a shell.

high_findings.title: Prompt Injection Attempt Detected
high_findings.description: SKILL.md instructs agents to avoid reading helper source before running it and to treat scripts as black boxes. This can suppress inspection of executable code before use.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The text directly tells the agent not to read source before executing helper scripts. It is suspicious in a marketplace skill because it can interfere with source inspection and safety review.

high_findings.title: User-Supplied Shell Command Execution
high_findings.description: with_server.py passes the --server argument to subprocess.Popen with shell=True. A crafted server string can execute arbitrary shell operations in the caller environment.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code explicitly uses shell=True with an argument supplied through the command line. The behavior is intentional, but it is still a clear arbitrary command execution surface.

medium_findings.title: Arbitrary Follow-Up Command Execution
medium_findings.description: with_server.py runs the remaining command-line arguments after servers are ready. This is expected for the tool, but it can execute unsafe commands if prompt input is untrusted.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command is executed without shell=True, which reduces injection risk. It still runs arbitrary user-selected programs as part of normal operation.

medium_findings.title: Browser Output Written to Local Filesystem
medium_findings.description: Example scripts write console logs and screenshots to local paths. This is useful for debugging but can store sensitive page content outside the project.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The writes are explicit and local, so this is not exfiltration. The risk is moderate because captured browser content can include private data.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Localhost URLs Are Benign Examples
low_findings.description: The detected URLs point to localhost and are used as example targets for local web testing. No external data exfiltration endpoint is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: All cited URLs use localhost and match the stated purpose of testing local web applications. I did not find evidence of remote collection or transmission.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The cited lines contain descriptive metadata and argparse option definitions, not weak hashing or cryptographic operations. No evidence found for cryptographic misuse.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines were reviewed directly and contain no cryptographic API usage. This is a scanner pattern mismatch.

dangerous_patterns.title: subprocess.Popen with shell=True
dangerous_patterns.description: The helper starts a server command through the shell, enabling shell metacharacter interpretation from the supplied command string.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The shell=True argument is visible in the subprocess.Popen call. This is a well-known command execution risk when command strings are not constrained.

dangerous_patterns.title: Instruction to Use Executable Scripts as Black Boxes
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill text encourages direct execution of bundled scripts while discouraging source review. This raises supply-chain review risk for marketplace users.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The wording is explicit and directly affects how an AI agent should handle bundled executable code. It is not proof of malware, but it is a strong safety concern.

content.user_title: Test Local Web Apps with Playwright
content.value_statement: Manual browser checks miss rendered-state issues and console errors. This skill guides Playwright scripts that inspect pages, capture screenshots, and run local servers.
actual_capabilities: Guides Python Playwright scripts for local browser automation.
Shows how to wait for rendered application state before inspection.
Captures browser screenshots and console logs for debugging.
Discovers visible buttons, links, inputs, and selectors on a page.
Provides a helper script for starting local servers before automation.
limitations: Requires Playwright and browser dependencies in the execution environment.
Targets local applications and static HTML, not hosted production monitoring.
The server helper can execute supplied shell commands.
Examples use fixed localhost URLs and local output paths.

use_cases.title: Verify a Frontend Change
use_cases.description: Run browser checks against a local dev server and confirm that key UI elements render correctly.

use_cases.title: Debug Client-Side Errors
use_cases.description: Capture console output and screenshots while reproducing a local application issue.

use_cases.title: Inspect Dynamic Page Structure
use_cases.description: Load a JavaScript application, wait for network idle, and identify selectors for follow-up automation.

prompt_templates.title: Check a Local Page
prompt_templates.prompt: Open my local app with Playwright, wait for it to finish loading, and report visible buttons, links, and inputs.

prompt_templates.title: Capture Debug Evidence
prompt_templates.prompt: Run a browser check against my local app, capture console messages, take a screenshot, and summarize visible errors.

prompt_templates.title: Test a User Flow
prompt_templates.prompt: Use Playwright to test the main login flow in my local app and report each step, observed result, and failure point.

prompt_templates.title: Coordinate Servers and Automation
prompt_templates.prompt: Start the required local frontend and backend servers, wait for readiness, run browser automation, and clean up the processes.

output_examples.input: Inspect the rendered homepage at my local development URL.
output: Found primary navigation, two call-to-action buttons, and one email input.
No console errors appeared after the page reached network idle.
A screenshot was captured for visual review.

output_examples.input: Debug why my dashboard button does not respond.
output: The dashboard button is visible, but the click did not trigger route navigation.
The console showed a client-side handler error after the click.
Recommended next step: inspect the click handler and route configuration.

best_practices: Review helper scripts before running them in a sensitive workspace.
Use local test data when capturing screenshots or console logs.
Wait for network idle or specific selectors before inspecting dynamic pages.
anti_patterns: Do not pass untrusted text directly into server command arguments.
Do not store screenshots or logs from pages that contain secrets.
Do not rely on DOM inspection before JavaScript rendering is complete.

faq.question: What does this skill help test?
faq.answer: It helps test local web applications with Playwright-driven browser automation, screenshots, console logs, and DOM inspection.

faq.question: Does it require a running server?
faq.answer: It can work with an existing server, static HTML, or a helper script that starts local servers before automation.

faq.question: Can it test production websites?
faq.answer: The examples and guidance are focused on local development workflows, not production monitoring.

faq.question: What should I review before using it?
faq.answer: Review any server commands and helper scripts because they can execute local processes.

faq.question: Does it capture browser logs?
faq.answer: Yes. The examples show how to collect console messages during Playwright automation.

faq.question: Is this safe for sensitive applications?
faq.answer: Use caution. Screenshots and logs can contain private data, and the helper can run supplied commands.
